text: The surgeon performs a left colectomy, focusing on the sigmoid colon, which shows signs of a previous perforation in the descending colon.
The surgical field shows the descending colon with visible signs of a previous perforation, as the surgeon performs a left colectomy for diverticular disease.

The surgeon performs high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) to facilitate length for resection. The dissection focuses on skeletonizing the tissue to clearly identify the IMA, preparing to use a vessel sealer.

The surgeon performs high ligations of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and vein (IMV) to facilitate length for resection. The dissection focuses on skeletonizing the tissue to clearly identify the IMA, followed by the use of a vessel sealer.

The surgeon follows the aorta upwards towards the DJ flexure, isolating the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). A medial to lateral dissection is performed, preparing the area for subsequent lateral mobilization.

The surgeon follows the aorta upwards towards the DJ flexure, isolating the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). A medial to lateral dissection is performed, preparing the area for subsequent lateral mobilization.

The surgeon isolates and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) to gain length due to the patient's short mesentery, while performing medial to lateral dissection. A sponge is placed to protect the ureter as the lateral dissection begins.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and resects it to gain additional length due to the patient's short mesentery. A sponge is placed to protect the ureter as the surgeon begins the lateral dissection of the mid descending colon.

The surgeon marks out the margins to maintain orientation during the procedure and begins lateral dissection in the area of a prior perforation in the descending colon. The medial plane is identified, and preparations are made for splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon marks the margins of the resection area to maintain orientation, identifying the medial plane near the site of a prior perforation. The lateral dissection is underway, with plans for splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon joins the previously medially dissected plane, facilitating the lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure. Using a vessel sealer for control and hemostasis, the surgeon exposes the left kidney and approaches the base of the pancreas.

The surgeon joins the previously medially dissected plane, facilitating the dissection near the spleen. A vessel sealer is used for control and hemostasis. The left kidney is fully exposed, and the dissection progresses towards the base of the pancreas.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the splenic flexure of the transverse colon, reducing tension for the upcoming anastomosis. The pancreas is exposed as the splenic flexure is fully mobilized. The surgeon performs distal mobilization, including a partial total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, to gain additional length over the sacral promontory.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the splenic flexure and transverse colon, reducing tension for the upcoming anastomosis. The flexure is fully mobilized, and the pancreas is exposed. The surgeon performs distal mobilization, including a partial total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection to gain additional length over the sacral promontory.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the rectum to enhance mobility for transrectal extraction of the specimen. The mesentery is cleared, and posterior dissection of the rectum is conducted to facilitate anastomosis and extraction.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the rectum to enhance its mobility for transrectal extraction. The mesentery is cleared, and posterior dissection of the rectum is conducted to facilitate anastomosis and extraction.

The surgeon cauterizes the proximal mesentery up to the planned margin, ensuring it is clear of any false ICG signals. The mesentery is dissected a few centimeters proximal to the thickened area observed earlier in the procedure. The proximal margin is then transected.

The surgeon cauterizes the proximal mesentery up to the planned margin, ensuring clear visualization of the area. The mesentery is cleared to avoid false indications of blood supply, marked by green ICG. The surgeon then proceeds to transect the proximal margin.
The surgeon prepares to transect the rectum, opting not to use a stapler for transrectal extraction in cases of benign disease. The surgical field shows the rectum being positioned for transection.
The surgeon prepares to transect the rectum, indicating a preference for not using a stapler for transrectal extraction in benign disease cases.

The surgeon uses scissors to divide the specimen into smaller pieces for extraction, as the vessel sealer struggles with thicker sections of the bowel. Each piece is individually removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses scissors and a vessel sealer to chop the specimen into smaller pieces for extraction, ensuring each piece is small enough to be removed individually. The process involves cutting and removing each piece sequentially.

The surgeon cleans the surgical field and prepares for a single-layer sewn anastomosis. A silk suture is used to stabilize the bowel, preventing movement during the procedure. The anastomosis begins posteriorly, sewing out and around the far corner, then proceeds to the near side. The surgeon checks perfusion to ensure good tissue viability and takes care to visualize and secure the sutures with precise bites.

The surgeon prepares for a single-layer sewn anastomosis by aligning the proximal and distal bowel segments with a silk suture to prevent movement. The anastomosis begins posteriorly, sewing out and around the far corner, then progresses to the near side. The surgeon checks perfusion to ensure adequate blood supply and takes care to visualize and secure good tissue bites.
The surgeon transitions to the canal, meticulously imbricating the anterior row of the anastomosis. Two sutures are used to ensure proper alignment of the tissue edges.
The surgeon transitions to the anterior row of the anastomosis, meticulously aligning the tissue and using two sutures to imbricate the anterior section, ensuring precise alignment.
The surgeon ties two sutures together to complete the sewn anastomosis, expressing satisfaction with the result. A leak test is then performed to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.
